questions.optionB: Guaranteeing a successful outcome to the patient
questions.explanation: Informed consent: valid consent requires — (1) Patient must be competent (sound mind, age ≥18), (2) Voluntary (no coercion/undue influence), (3) Informed (disclosure of nature of procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives, consequences of refusal). Doctor is NOT obligated to guarantee outcome. Written consent preferred for surgical/invasive procedures. Implied consent: unconscious emergency patient (doctrine of necessity). Consent by proxy: for minors (parents/guardian), mentally incompetent. Section 92 IPC: good faith. Reference: Reddy — Consent.

questions.questionText: Therapeutic privilege allows a doctor to:
questions.optionB: Withhold information from a patient when full disclosure would be detrimental to the patient's health
questions.explanation: Therapeutic privilege: exception to informed consent — doctor may withhold certain information if they believe full disclosure would seriously harm the patient's physical or mental health (e.g., terminal diagnosis in fragile patient). Must be exercised judiciously and in patient's best interest. Other exceptions to consent: emergency (unconscious patient), statutory requirement (notifiable diseases, court order). Doctrine of double effect: treatment that may cause harm as side effect of beneficial purpose. Reference: Reddy — Consent Exceptions.

questions.optionB: Duty of care, breach of duty, causation (breach caused harm), and damage to patient (4 D's)
questions.explanation: Medical negligence (4 D's): (1) DUTY: doctor-patient relationship established, (2) DERELICTION: breach of standard of care (Bolam test — standard of reasonable body of medical opinion), (3) DIRECT CAUSATION: dereliction directly caused injury ('but for' test), (4) DAMAGE: actual harm/injury to patient. Civil negligence: tort law (compensation). Criminal negligence: gross negligence → Sec 304A IPC (death by negligence). Res ipsa loquitur: 'the thing speaks for itself' (e.g., sponge left in abdomen). Reference: Reddy — Medical Negligence.

questions.questionText: Under the Consumer Protection Act (CPA), a doctor can be sued for:
questions.optionB: Deficiency of service (medical negligence — patient is a 'consumer' of medical services)
questions.explanation: Consumer Protection Act 2019: medical services come under 'service.' Patient = consumer. Deficiency of service = medical negligence. Can file complaint in Consumer Forum (district/state/national). Exception: free treatment in government hospital (no consideration paid). Limitation: complaints within 2 years. NCDRC: National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (>₹10 crore). Supreme Court: appeal against NCDRC. Indian Medical Council (Professional Conduct, Etiquette, and Ethics) Regulations 2002: professional code. Reference: Reddy — CPA.

questions.questionText: The document issued by a registered medical practitioner stating the cause of death is called:
questions.optionB: Death certificate (Form 4/4A under Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969)
questions.explanation: Death certificate: issued by attending physician/RMP who treated the patient during last illness. Mandatory under Registration of Births and Deaths Act 1969. Form 4 (institutional deaths), Form 4A (non-institutional). Must state cause of death (immediate, antecedent, underlying). Medico-legal cases: death certificate after post-mortem by autopsy surgeon. Inquest: investigation into cause of death held by magistrate (Sec 176 CrPC) or police (Sec 174 CrPC). Reference: Reddy — Death Certificate.
